title: Acute Hematogenous Osteomyelitis
chief_complaint: My son has a fever for 5 days and won't walk on his right leg. He says his knee hurts. He's been limping for 2 days and now he refuses to put any weight on it. He's never had anything like this before.

physical_exam.Right Leg: Point tenderness over distal femoral metaphysis (just above knee joint). Warmth and swelling. Refuses to move knee joint (pseudoparalysis). Erythema mild. No fluctuance.
physical_exam.Knee: Mild effusion (sympathetic). Range of motion severely limited by pain.
physical_exam.Other: No other foci of infection. No murmur (rules out endocarditis).

hidden_data.mri: Distal femoral metaphysis: Marrow edema on T2/STIR. Subperiosteal abscess (2cm) along medial cortex. No joint involvement (differentiates from septic arthritis).
hidden_data.blood_cx: Methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) - most common pathogen
hidden_data.xray: Normal on presentation (XR lags 7-10 days behind infection).
hidden_data.aspiration: CT-guided aspiration of subperiosteal abscess: Purulent material. Gram-positive cocci in clusters. Culture: MSSA.

correct_diagnosis: Acute Hematogenous Osteomyelitis (Distal Femoral Metaphysis) with Subperiosteal Abscess due to MSSA
key_learning_points: Acute hematogenous osteomyelitis = most common in children <10 years. Metaphysis of long bones (distal femur, proximal tibia) most common - rich but sluggish blood supply

Pathogenesis: Hematogenous seeding of metaphyseal vessels → bacterial proliferation → inflammation → abscess formation → subperiosteal spread (periosteum loosely attached in children) → bone necrosis (sequestrum)

Classic: Fever + Point tenderness over metaphysis + Refusal to walk (pseudoparalysis) + Elevated CRP/ESR. XR normal early (takes 10-14 days for periosteal reaction). MRI gold standard for early diagnosis
Most common organism: S. aureus (80-90%). MSSA > MRSA. Kingella kingae in children <4 years (fastidious - requires special cultures or PCR). Salmonella in sickle cell disease

Treatment: IV antibiotics for 2-4 weeks then oral for 2-4 weeks (total 4-6 weeks). MSSA: Cefazolin or Oxacillin IV. MRSA: Vancomycin or Clindamycin (if susceptible). Surgical drainage if abscess >2cm or not responding to antibiotics in 48h. Serial CRP to monitor response
